title: Bram Moolenaar (1961–2023)
subtitle: The maintainer who made Vim what it is
summary: Bram Moolenaar wrote Vim and maintained it almost single-handedly for 32 years. Vim was, in many ways, one person's life work. Worth knowing about that person.

blocks.text: Vim is a piece of software written and maintained by one person for the better part of three decades. That is unusual. Most software of Vim's reach — used by millions of developers, packaged on every major operating system, baked into every Linux distribution — is the work of large teams. Vim is the work of Bram Moolenaar, with help from a community of contributors he reviewed personally for thirty-two years.

Bram was a Dutch software engineer who started working on Vim — "Vi IMitation" at the time — in 1988 to use on his Amiga. The first public release was in 1991. He renamed it "Vi IMproved" once it had grown features that `vi` proper lacked: *visual mode*, multi-level undo, syntax highlighting, a real scripting language, plugin loading, multi-window support, and dozens of other things.

He kept improving Vim continuously from 1991 until his death in August 2023, including major releases — Vim 7 (2006), Vim 8 (2016), and Vim 9 (2022) — that added scripting language overhauls, channels and jobs, terminal integration, popup windows, and a fully redesigned Vim9script with proper compilation. Through all of this Bram was the canonical reviewer of every patch. Most days he answered email about Vim before he ate breakfast. He wrote large parts of the documentation himself. He once said that Vim was a hobby project he had been polishing for too long to stop.

Bram was also a tireless advocate for ICCF Holland, a charity supporting children in Uganda. The standard Vim startup screen used to display "Help poor children in Uganda!" with a link to the charity. The {key::}{key:help} {key::}{key:iccf} text in Vim still leads to those donation pages.

It is easy, looking at Vim's pile of documentation and accumulated commands, to assume the design was haphazard. The opposite is true. Bram was extremely conservative about adding features and ruthlessly consistent about extending existing patterns rather than introducing new ones. The fact that Vim's grammar still composes the way it did in 1991 — that {key:d}{key:i}{key:w} works on text objects from plugins published in 2024 — is downstream of one engineer's taste and patience over thirty-two years.

Look closely at any feature that other editors copied later: text objects, the {key:.} command, registers, marks, the operator/motion model. They came together because Bram had a strong sense of what fit the existing grammar and what didn't, and would say no to features that didn't compose.

Vim is now maintained by a small team of long-time contributors — Christian Brabandt has taken on the principal-maintainer role — who continue to merge patches and ship releases. Vim 9 is being polished. Bug fixes still land. The project is in steady state.

Neovim, started by community members in 2014 partly to enable a more open development model, is the more active fork in 2025. But every keystroke you learn from this book was, at some level, designed or approved by Bram. When you {key:d}{key:i}{key:w} a word, you are using a feature he reviewed and merged into a codebase he stewarded for the entire post-Cold-War era.

blocks.title: The Charity
blocks.text: ICCF Holland — Bram's chosen charity — supports orphans of the AIDS pandemic in Uganda by funding schooling, food, and medical care. After Bram's death the Vim project continued to point to ICCF, and the foundation has been the recipient of many tributes from the broader open-source community. Type {key::}{key:help} {key::}{key:uganda} in Vim to see Bram's original pitch in his own words. If Vim has saved you years of editing time over your career, ICCF is the canonical place to send some money.
